title: Conversations with Hispanic/Latino Community Members about How to Share Firearm Suicide Resources and Facilitate Research Engagement Through an Online Information Hub: Procedures and Principles for a Formative Evaluation Project
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"d62i3","text":"The overarching goal of this formative evaluation project was to provide insights for the creation of an online information hub and \"collaboratory\" for suicide and firearm mortality information, resources, and research engagement opportunities in Hispanic/Latino communities in Utah. To achieve this goal, the following procedures were used to collect information from community members on how to best: (1) share firearm suicide information and prevention resources and (2) facilitate future firearm suicide prevention research opportunities with Hispanic/Latino community members through a new information hub and \"collaboratory\". We followed principles from the World Café model, a validated participatory approach used to facilitate open discussions in small groups for the purpose of accessing collective intelligence and uncovering deeper insights on a given topic. Colloquially, we refer to the following steps as Community Awareness, empoweRment, and Empathy (CARE) Café procedures.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":681,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":920,"length":58}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"flb38","text":"BACKGROUND","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"22f3h","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"23vuk","text":"The overarching goal of this formative evaluation project was to provide insights for the creation of an online information hub and \"collaboratory\" for suicide and firearm mortality information, resources, and research engagement opportunities in Hispanic/Latino communities in Utah. To achieve this goal, the following procedures were used to collect information from community members on how to best: (1) share firearm suicide information and prevention resources and (2) facilitate future firearm suicide prevention research opportunities with Hispanic/Latino community members through a new information hub and \"collaboratory\". ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9i6qo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1fglv","text":"Grounded in the philosophies of community-based evaluation1,2 and cultural humility,3 the evaluation procedures outlined below represent the culmination of a collaborative, community-centered effort during which partners from the University of Utah and Elevate Utah (formerly Centro Hispano) worked hand-in-hand to gather insights that are relevant at a local scale, account for local context, and ideally enhance the inclusion of on-the-ground expertise for the creation of a new information hub and \"collaboratory\" resource online. For this project, we aimed to engage community members who are often not well represented in public health program/infrastructure decision-making. We sought to engage local community members to raise awareness about an important public health topic (i.e., the intersection of self-harm and firearm violence), empathize with community members affected by the topic in their community, and empower community members to provide real-world insights on how to best share information and resources on firearm-involved suicide through a new online information hub and \"collaboratory\".","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":58,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":84,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"asp3j","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f2a6u","text":"We followed principles from the World Café model,4,5 a validated participatory approach used to facilitate open discussions in small groups for the purpose of accessing collective intelligence and uncovering deeper insights on a given topic. Colloquially, we refer to the following information collection steps as Community Awareness, empoweRment, and Empathy (CARE) Café procedures.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":49,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":314,"length":58}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fq1tf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2bugu","text":"Note: The following procedures were used to collect information from community members in response to the specific evaluation project goal stated above. The procedures are project-specific and were not formally validated. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":222}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ctv5i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a8da0","text":"CONCEPTUAL NOTES","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c3v9m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5ie2e","text":"World Café ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11},{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4obg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9it69","text":"Developed by Brown and Isaacs in the 1990s, the World Café model is a participatory approach to enriching conversations, generating creative ideas, and facilitating collective learning.6 The World Café format is inherently flexible, and creativity and improvisation are core foundations of the approach,7 permitting it to be adapted to many different circumstances. Over time, the World Café model has been adapted and used in different contexts spanning business, community, and academia. The World Café approach is guided by core principles, which include:6,7 (1) Having a clear purpose for convening community members; (2) Creating a hospitable, safe, and comfortable space; (3) Exploring questions that matter to the participants; (4) Encouraging and valuing everyone’s participation and allowing for different forms of participation; (5) Connecting diverse perspectives; (6) Listening together for relevant insights; and (7) Sharing discoveries. Logistically, the World Café approach is intentionally grounded in the belief that the best ideas and solutions often emerge not from formal processes but from informal ones, such as over a meal. For these reasons, the World Café model necessitates the creation of an environment that evokes a feeling of both informality and intimacy, which can be created using tablecloths, flowers, and light music to convey a café-like atmosphere.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":185,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":304,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":559,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"akpp0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ago4d","text":"Of relevance to our project, recent studies have demonstrated that World Café approach is well-suited for collecting qualitative insights5,8 and may promote resource efficiency (e.g., collecting information on attitudes and perceptions from a large group over a short period of time), dialogue facilitation, and mutual learning.5 Moreover, the World Café approach has been successfully applied to the fields of health promotion and prevention, as previous studies have demonstrated the usefulness of the World Café approach for collecting participant insights and exploring understudied public health topics.8 Notably, the World Café approach is built on an assumption that people have within them the wisdom to collectively confront even the most difficult social challenges – and that the answers to those challenges lie within their lived experiences.4 ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":137,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":328,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":608,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":855,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"63aho","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"coe00","text":"Cultural Humility","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17},{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5jqta","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dlsuo","text":"Cultural humility was born out of an understanding that sociocultural mismatches between healthcare professionals and the people they serve clinically – including a lack of knowledge about persons’ life experiences and health beliefs – can be detrimental to providing appropriate health services or achieving optimal wellbeing. In healthcare, cultural humility has been espoused as a useful therapeutic framework for mental healthcare professionals to meaningfully engage clients across cultural backgrounds, create social bonds, and develop openness to discussing matters related to mental health.10,11  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":598,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fno64","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aptin","text":"The ethos of cultural humility motivated the procedures below. As firearm suicide incidence increases in Hispanic/Latino communities,12,13 there is a need for developing and disseminating culturally responsive resources alongside Hispanic/Latino community members,14 highlighting the nuanced ways that Hispanic/Latino individuals understand, experience, and talk about suicide and firearm safety within their communities. The following procedures prioritized listening and signaling to community members that their perspectives are valued, thereby cultivating respect and creating an atmosphere that empowered community members to share their lived expertise and needs (e.g., 15) in relation to informing the creation of a new online information hub. This project also prioritized engagement activities to be held at a community location, where community members were most likely to feel comfortable engaging in these processes.9   ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":133,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":264,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":676,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":928,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"c2g74","text":"Note: The following procedures were used to collect information from community members in response to the specific evaluation project goal stated below. The procedures are project-specific and were not formally validated. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":222}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2vk36","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Administrative Preparation

Convene a formal Planning Team to help delegate responsibility for the procedures described below. For example, in our project, the Planning Team was co-led by the Project/Evaluation Lead and an executive leader (e.g., Executive Director or Chief Executive Officer) from a community organization (hereinafter "community partner") serving as the Community Lead. Early into our collaboration, the Project/Evaluation Lead and Community Lead brainstormed resources and expenditures necessary for executing World-Café-inspired community engagement sessions. Resources included food, supplies, promotional materials, and event space to create an environment that will nurture feelings of informality, intimacy, and respect for the participating community members (i.e., per World Café principles). 4 Following a community-engaged approach with organizational partnership, empower the Community Lead to make planning decisions based on their expertise and knowledge of the community being served, 9 thereby adhering to the ethos of (1) respecting the socio-cultural context within which an organization operates and (2) distributing decision-making to align the group’s activities with the needs and expectations of the community being served. 15
Planning Team

From the community partner, invite a Spanish-speaking (i.e., bi- or multilingual) colleague with face-to-face community health, health education and promotion, social support, or patient advocacy experience, such as a community health worker (CHW) or social worker, to join the Planning Team and serve as Café Facilitator.
During the first meeting of the full Planning Team (i.e., Project/Evaluation Lead, Community Lead, and Café Facilitator), recap the purpose of the Café event.

Schedule recurring meetings to establish consistent, routine project check-ins leading up to the CARE Café session. Based on the principles of cultural humility, prioritize meeting times that work best for the community partner, as well as virtual meetings. Doing so will respect of the Community Lead and Café Facilitator’s work schedules, e.g., prioritizing existing community member activities, the Café Facilitator’s field work, and the community partner’s existing community event schedule.
Scheduling and Location

Schedule the CARE Café session 8-10 weeks in advance. This will give the Planning Team ample time to prepare for hosting a Café session, including promoting the Café, ordering food, acquiring event supplies, identifying and printing suicide and firearm mortality prevention resources, and preparing the Café script and discussion questions.

Prioritize having the community partner choose the appropriate timing and location of a CARE Café session, given their local expertise and understanding of (1) the community being served (e.g., the community’s health-related priorities and knowledge about participation barriers, such as transportation) and (2) their organization’s existing priorities and responsibilities.

When scheduling a CARE Café session and choosing an event location, respect and prioritize the Community Lead’s expertise and prior experience from other events attended by the community. 9,10 A CARE Café session may not be well attended or successful if scheduled in a manner that is not respectful of community members’ availability and responsibilities. Moreover, a CARE Café session may not be well attended if held at a location that is inaccessible or unfamiliar to community members. For example, in our project, the Planning Team decided that Elevate Utah’s on-site community education room would be an ideal CARE Café session location – a familiar space in the community often used for activities, events, trainings, and resource sharing.

Prioritize the Community Lead and Café Facilitator’s expertise for identifying times that would work well for inviting adults in the community and facilitating their participation. For example, in our project, a CARE Café session was scheduled during the late summer, before the start of the school year. The timing of this Café session was chosen because the Community Lead expressed experience with Hispanic/Latino adults in their community being more engaged with community events during back-to-school time but less likely to attend events following the start of a new school year (e.g., due to school-related responsibilities and after-school activities).
Café Session Size

Determine the appropriate number of Café session attendees. For example, in our project, we made the early decision to host approximately 15-20 community members per CARE Café session based on the community partner’s guidance on (1) the optimal number of participants for a meaningful engagement activity within the local Hispanic/Latino community and (2) potential venue constraints.
Promotion

Create flyers promoting the CARE Café session 6-8 weeks in advance. Ensure that all necessary details are depicted accurately. Include (1) information about the purpose of the session, noting it to be an informal, voluntary gathering to be held over a meal for the purpose of discussing how to better share firearm suicide resources and facilitate future research engagement through the creation of a new online information hub; (2) an invitation to participate using phrases intended to uplift and empower community members to connect through their lived experiences; (3) information about the meal and attendee compensation; (4) a method for registering confidentially; and (5) time and location details. Use free resources such as Flesch Reading Ease and Gunning Fog Index tools online to maximize the text’s readability while maintaining key information. Ask staff from the community partner to revise and edit the flyers and add their organization’s branding elements (e.g., logos, fonts, and color schemes). This will help the flyers resemble the materials typically shared by the organization. These design edits may also help communicate a sense of trust and familiarity to potential Café session attendees, i.e., meeting psychological norms about how the organization’s flyers ought to look and what community members ought to expect from such an event, thereby helping to create the comfortable and familiar environment espoused by the World Café approach. 4

Although multilingual promotional materials can be created using several methods, including certified translators, fluent speakers, and generative artificial intelligence tools (e.g., DEEPL or ChatGPT), prioritize the community partner giving final input on promotional material phrasing/terminology and language, given their local expertise and knowledge of the community being served. For example, in our project, Elevate Utah identified the most useful promotion methods – including Facebook, Instagram, and word-of-mouth –based on their expertise with the community being served (e.g., 15 ).
Facilitation Guide and Discussion Questions

Draft a Café session facilitation guide 3 weeks before the event. This will give the Planning Team time to finalize discussion questions, practice the timing of the sessions, and ensure the script is conversational and informal (i.e., aligned with World Café principles).

In the facilitation guide, outline the Café session run-of-show (i.e., what will happen and when during the Café) and a verbatim script of what would be said. The use of a written facilitation guide will help ensure the Café session is well-organized and completed within the time scheduled. The guide will also help the Café Facilitator remember to communicate all relevant information and consistently ask the discussion questions across all Café sessions hosted on the same topic. For example, in our project, the facilitation guide included four sections and the anticipated time for completing each section: (1) Welcome (2 minutes), (2) Topic Overview (3 minutes), (3) Discussion Rules (2 minutes), (4) Establishing Empathy & Asking Questions (~100 minutes), (5) Closing (5 minutes, as needed). Each section served the following purposes: Welcome: To welcome the attendees; invite the attendees to take food, if they have not yet done so; introduce the Planning Team, Café Facilitator, and the Café Facilitator’s background and role with the community partner, beginning to build trust with the person leading the Café session; and describe the purpose of the Café (i.e., discussing how to better share firearm suicide resources and facilitate future research engagement to inform the creation of a new online information hub). Topic Overview: To introduce the topics of suicide and firearm-involved suicide death in the U.S. and in Hispanic/Latino populations nationally and locally, and describe additional informational resources available to the attendees. Discussion Rules: To set expectations for the Café session discussion. The facilitation guide describes the intent to have a friendly, informal discussion, where attendees can come together to eat, drink coffee, and share ideas. The facilitation guide invites attendees to be comfortable sharing their thoughts, that their voices matter, and that there are no right or wrong answers to any of the forthcoming discussion questions. The facilitation guide invites attendees to feel comfortable sharing their views, even if those views differ from the views of other attendees. The facilitation guide encourages attendees to be respectful of others' perspectives and experiences. Establishing Empathy & Asking Questions: To stimulate an active conversation in response to discussion questions. Voluntary: To give attendees time and space to share personal stories related to suicide prevention or firearm-related injuries. Closing: To invite attendees to share any additional, lingering thoughts regarding the discussion questions, and thank the attendees before adjourning the Café session.
Create a timing document to accompany the facilitation guide, which will help the Café Facilitator progress through the run-of-show in a timely fashion.

Collectively draft and revise discussion questions to include in the facilitation guide, e.g., questions asked to elicit the attendees’ perspectives on how best to share firearm-involved suicide prevention resources. Include open-ended questions to encourage attendees to share insights, stories, examples, and perspectives from their lived experiences and social realities. 17 Similar to focus groups, the Café sessions will ideally help the Planning Team leverage group dynamics to generate insights, allowing for depth through discussion, and synergistic idea generation and revelation. 18 For example, in our project, as is a best practice with focus groups, market research, and other data collection methods, 16 dichotomous, yes-or-no questions were avoided, as we prioritized discussion and emergent ideas. Select a limited number of discussion questions to allow for conversation. i.e., 2-4 questions. For example, from our project: "With the input you share today, we will create an online information center for sharing resources and research in the community. What would be the most useful ways to share resources about firearm safety and suicide prevention with you? "What do you consider to be useful ways to share information about opportunities for you to participate in research studies on suicide prevention?"

After an initial drafting of the discussion questions and facilitation guide, empower the Café Facilitator to review and edit the facilitation guide and discussion questions. The Café Facilitator also translates the facilitation guide into Spanish (if it was not originally written in Spanish), adjusting the script to be more conversational.
Food and Supplies

Quality food and beverages are important for achieving several core World Café elements, i.e., the belief that the best ideas and solutions often emerge not from formal processes but from informal ones, such as a conversation held over a meal or coffee, in a hospitable setting that evokes feelings of comfort and respect. 4 Moreover, tablecloths, flowers, and light music convey an actual café-like atmosphere. 4

Given their local expertise and understanding of the community being served, prioritize the community partner providing recommendations on which local restaurants/vendors and foods and beverages should be considered.

To ensure the preferred restaurant is available, place the catering order 3 weeks before the Café session. Day-of delivery options help the Planning Team focus on setting up the Café session and greeting Café session attendees instead of collecting and setting up a large food order.

Two weeks before the Café session, acquire tablecloths, name tags, markers (for writing names), facial tissue packages for each Café table (for anyone who may become emotional); tableware, eating utensils, and food serving utensils; chocolates (for comfort); and translucent stand-up sign holders for each Café session table (e.g., 8.5 x 11 inches or 8.5 x 5.5 inches for displaying Café session information, reminders, or suicide prevention resources).
Purchase live plants or high-quality, reusable artificial flowers and potted plants one week before the Café session.

Acquire compensation for Café session attendees’ time. Given their local expertise and understanding of the community being served, ask the community partner to provide recommendations on what type of compensation would be best, i.e., if gift cards, which store would be most useful in the community being served.
Informational Resources

Prepare informational resources about firearm safety and suicide prevention. For example, in our project, given the attendees’ potential experiences with firearm violence and suicide, as well as the rising incidence of firearm suicide deaths in our local community, it was imperative to provide any available, relevant resources. These included printed versions of suicide prevention information from the Suicide Crisis Helpline (i.e., 988) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in Spanish and English, as well as copies of firearm safety and storage aids. Additionally, we provide information about local mental health and primary care providers (e.g., Federally Qualified Health Centers), including contact information for local community mental health agencies specializing in care for Hispanic/Latino adults.
Registration Reminders

Café registration and attendance reminders should be confidentially communicated to all session registrants 24 hours before the Café session. The registration reminder should provide timely logistical information (e.g., parking instructions) and contact information for participants to contact with questions.
Conducting a CARE Café session consists of five phases, colloquially referred to as Community, Awareness, empoweRment, and Empathy, which follow a Setup phase.
Setup
On the day of the Café session, arrive at the Café session venue 1-2 hours before the start of the Café session. This will provide ample time to set up and prepare for welcoming the attendees.
Schedule the food, coffee, and other beverages to arrive and be set up 30 minutes before the start of the Café session.

Arrange tables and chairs to promote close interactions among the attendees. Adorn each table with a tablecloth. For our project, we added light purple table runners. Light purple was chosen for its association with suicide awareness (e.g., as depicted on the suicide awareness ribbon), tranquility, and peace. 19

Decorate each table with the flowers/potted plants, facial tissue packets, chocolates, and suicide prevention and firearm safety resources – all to signal our respect for topics being explored and provide useful resources.
Distribute blank name tags, markers, and any additional information at each seat (e.g., information reiterating the purpose of the Café session, consent cover letter).

Display the translucent sign holders with a message welcoming the attendees, encouraging attendees to help themselves to food and beverages at any time during the Café session, and encouraging the attendees to introduce themselves to the people at their table. The sign holders may also be used to display a question for attendees to reflect on and discuss at their tables before the start of the session (i.e., a “brainstorming reflection question”). For example, in our project, the brainstorming reflection question was, “What types of resources or information about suicide prevention and firearm safety would be most helpful to you?”

Display additional printed informational resources on a table in the back of the room, e.g., Suicide Crisis Helpline (i.e., 988), firearm safety and storage aids, and the location and contact information for local community mental health agencies and primary care providers specializing in care for Hispanic/Latino adults.
Situate the Café Facilitator at the head of the room with a printed script and laptop, so they can step into a space encircled by all the tables and closely interact with all attendees.

Community (a.k.a., Breaking Bread) Begin the Café session with the Community (or Breaking Bread ) phase. The objectives of this component are to establish a hospitable, safe, and comfortable space; build mutual respect for everyone involved in the Café; describe the purpose for convening community members; and encourage everyone’s participation, signaling that the Planning Team values the attendees time and participation. 4,7,8
As Café session attendees arrived, welcome and invite them to sit with other people to connect and introduce themselves to one another.
Invite attendees to help themselves to food, coffee, and other beverages.

After 10-15 minutes of eating and informal conversation, begin the formal Café session program using the script outlined in the facilitation guide (e.g., beginning with the Welcome section). Introduce the Planning Team, Café Facilitator, and the Café Facilitator’s background and role with the community partner, beginning to build trust with the person leading the Café session.

Encourage the attendees to continue eating while the formal program begins, which is consistent with the original World Café methodology and helps ensure that the CARE Café session is hospitable and informal.

Awareness Transition to the Awareness component, which is initiated when the Café Facilitator begins the Topic Overview section in the facilitation guide. This component establishes the foundation for meaningful Café dialogue: It creates a shared understanding of the scope and impact of firearm-involved suicide in Hispanic/Latino communities, demonstrates commitment to the attendee community's well-being, and signals to the attendees the intent to explore questions that are relevant for their community’s well-being.

Introduce the topics of suicide and firearm-involved suicide death in the U.S. broadly and among Hispanic/Latino populations specifically. Use handouts and an orated description of the epidemiology and public health impact of firearm-involved suicide death nationally and locally (e.g., include current data and statistics on suicide and firearm suicide among Hispanic/Latino adults).

Discuss information about available firearm mortality and suicide prevention resources in the community, including local mental health and primary care providers and healthcare agencies specializing in care for Hispanic/Latino adults.
Introduce the informational resources available in the room, e.g., the printed resources at their seats and the table at the back of the room.

Empathy Transition to the Empathy component. Given the potential emotional nature of the topics being explored, the purpose of the Empathy component is to connect diverse perspectives, 4 describe that you are creating a space to share lived experiences and opinions related to suicide and firearm mortality in the community, and foster mutual respect among people with similar sociocultural circumstances, thereby promoting empathy among everyone involved in the Café session.

Begin with the Discussion Rules section in the facilitation guide. This includes setting expectations for the Café session discussion. Describe the intent to have a friendly, respectful, and informal-but-constructive discussion while coming together to eat food and drink coffee (or other beverages).

Encourage attendees to feel comfortable sharing their thoughts and perspectives. Assure the attendees that their voices matter and that there are no right or wrong answers to any of the discussion questions.
Invite attendees to feel comfortable sharing their views, even if those views differ from the views of other attendees. Ask the attendees to remain respectful of others' perspectives and experiences.

Voluntary: In our project, a voluntary empathy-facilitating activity provided an opportunity to share and express oneself among peers, if attendees felt comfortable doing so. It is strongly recommended that this activity be conducted only when the facilitator or other present Planning Team members have health education and promotion, social support, patient advocacy, case management, or suicide gatekeeper training (e.g., Question, Persuade, Refer [QPR]). For this voluntary activity, provide a specified amount of time (e.g., 20-30 minutes) to offer attendees space to share experiences or stories related to suicide prevention or firearm-related mortality from their community. Before beginning this empathy-facilitating activity, establish the expectation that the CARE Café session is a space for openness. Invite attendees to feel comfortable taking 1-3 minutes to share what comes to mind. Reiterate that suicide and firearm mortality can be evocative public health topics, and no one should feel pressure to share their stories if they do not feel comfortable doing so. The 1-3-minute time limit helps set an expectation that you want to allow everyone to say something; however, the Café Facilitator should not interrupt any attendee who is sharing their story. Nevertheless, if an attendee loses track of time (e.g., 4+ minutes), the Café Facilitator should respectfully and politely redirect the conversation by acknowledging and thanking the attendee for their story at an appropriate pause, then ask if anyone else wants to share their thoughts. Although a voluntary component, the purpose of this activity is to create space for facilitating the exchange of emotional understanding, foster mutual respect and empowerment among people with similar sociocultural experiences, help participants find strength and encouragement in shared experiences, and establish empathy among persons. The Café Facilitator may also note that recalling and sharing experiences may have the power to help each other generate ideas on how to improve suicide prevention resource sharing and accessibility in the community.

empoweRment Transition to the empoweRment component, which continues with the Establishing Empathy & Asking Questions section in the facilitation guide. The empoweRment component represents the culmination of the Community, Awareness, and Empathy components, which began to raise awareness about suicide and firearm mortality, establish a sense of trust and togetherness among the Café session attendees, and energize the attendees to provide perspective on how to best promote resources and facilitate future research opportunities. The objectives of this component are to respond to open-ended questions, listen and respond to each other for relevant insights, and share input in relation to informing the creation of the new online information hub.

The empoweRment component is centered on the discussion questions. Begin the first question by briefly restating the goal of the Café session, i.e., to discuss how to better share firearm suicide resources and facilitate future research engagement through the creation of a new online information hub.
Allow 20-30 minutes of discussion time for each question.

Ask each question aloud, then encourage attendees to raise their hands to begin a conversation. The Café Facilitator should guide the discussion, maintaining the 20-30-minute discussion limit for each question while ensuring every participant has an opportunity to share their perspective.
Gently redirect the conversation when needed and acknowledge emotional responses with sensitivity and respect, thus maintaining a safe and non-judgmental environment for conversation.
Employ active listening techniques, summarizing key points to confirm understanding and validate participants' contributions to the community-engaged process.
Consistent with the principles of cultural humility, 9,10 do not try to guide or sway the attendees into talking about any specific idea. The attendees’ emergent ideas and reflections matter most.

Use the structured prompts to initiate or even deepen the conversation around specific aspects of each question. This can help draw out nuanced insights that might otherwise have remained unexpressed.
To conclude the Café session, after all discussion questions have been explored, invite the attendees to share final ideas or reflections.
Conclude the session with an expression of gratitude for everyone's openness and wisdom while reemphasizing the availability of existing community resources for suicide prevention and firearm safety.
Provide the attendee compensation.
Encourage attendees to take any remaining food and beverage with them.
Reiterate that the online information hub will be shared with the community and through the community partner once it has been created.

Reiterate the purpose of the Café session, i.e., to discuss how to better share firearm suicide resources and facilitate future research engagement through the creation of a new online information hub.
Reiterate that the attendees' insights will help inform the creation of a new online information hub.

Complete all necessary financial requirements before setting a date for the CARE Café session. 6 For example, in our project, the Project/Evaluation Lead and Community Lead executed a vendor contract 12 weeks before the CARE Café. The purpose of the vendor contract was to share financial support with Elevate Utah from a grant supporting the project. The 12-week lead time and resultant vendor contract allowed for time to build community among the two organizations and community members, promote the Café session to potential attendees, prepare the event space, create Café promotional materials and promote the session, conduct translation services, and facilitate the Cafés (i.e., engaging Elevate Utah staff). The contract outlined project expectations and provided infrastructure for planning and executing a Café session.

Plan and execute all appropriate and necessary regulatory requirements and ethical considerations, including ensuring attendee respect, beneficence, informed consent and voluntary participation, attendee confidentiality, and data protection before conducting the CARE Café session. Formally consult with the appropriate Institutional Review Board.

Draft structured prompts to initiate or even deepen the conversation around specific aspects of each question. This can help draw out nuanced insights that might otherwise have remained unexpressed. The structured prompts can be relevant to a priori knowledge about strategies that may help improve resource and research dissemination through an online information hub. For example, from our project: "To further the conversation, some ideas about possible ways to share information or resources online include: How-to guides, social media pages/channels, newsletters, ... , educational videos."
creator: Evan Goldstein
